name: cogsworth

metadata.name: cogsworth
metadata.summary: A framework for performing self-consistent population synthesis and orbital integration

documentation:
cogsworth

cogsworth is a Python package for performing seamlessly self-consistent population synthesis and galactic dynamics simulations!

### Installation A quick and easy `pip install cogsworth` will cover most use cases, but we recommend using a conda environment as well. Check out the full instructions [here](https://cogsworth.readthedocs.io/en/latest/pages/install.html). ### Documentation You can learn all about cogsworth from our collection of tutorials, examples and in-depth case studies on our online documentation [here](https://cogsworth.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html).

functions.name: cogsworth.events.identify_events
documentation:
Identify any events that occur in the stellar evolution that would affect the galactic evolution

:::{note}
This function currently only considers supernovae when identifying events
:::

# Parameters

p

: A `cogsworth` population

# Returns

primary_events_list

: A list of events for each bound binary or primary star. `None` is returned if there are no pertinent
  events, otherwise a list of dicts

secondary_events_list

: As `primary_events_lists` but for disrupted secondaries

functions.name: cogsworth.utils.translate_COSMIC_tables
documentation:
Translate COSMIC BSE tables to human-readable labels

For a full description of the translations, see the tables in the {mod}`~cogsworth.utils` module page.

# Parameters

tab

: Evolution table from COSMIC

kstars

: Whether to translate kstar_1 and kstar_2, by default True

evol_type

: Whether to translate evol_type, by default True

label_type

: Type of label (either "short" or "long"), by default "short"

replace_columns

: Whether to replace original columns (if not new ones are appended), by default True

# Returns

translated_tab

: The translated table

methods.name: cogsworth.tests.test_kicks.Test.test_duplicated_timesteps
documentation:
Ensure that the locations at timesteps in orbits are never duplicated

Motivated by a bug that occurred during the stitching of timesteps in the orbit of a kicked binary,
so that the orbit was not continuous. This test ensures that the orbit is continuous and only tests
the kicked binaries since the rest are unaffected

methods.name: cogsworth.tests.test_kicks.Test.test_saved_inc_phase
documentation:
Test that the inclination and phase are saved correctly in the kick events - such that the same
population at present day is fully recreated

functions.name: cogsworth.classify.determine_final_classes
documentation:
Determine the classes of each member of a population at the last point in the evolution (usually
present day).

Either supply a {class}`~cogsworth.pop.Population` class or each individual table separately

# Parameters

population

: A full population class created from the pop module, by default None

bpp

: Evolutionary history of each binary

initC

: Initial conditions for each binary

kick_info

: Information about the kicks that occur for each binary

orbits

: The orbits of each binary within the galaxy. Disrupted binaries should have two entries
  (for both stars), where the secondary is appended in sequence to the full list.

galactic_potential

: Galactic potential to use for evolving the orbits of binaries

# Returns

classes

: A DataFrame with a boolean column for each class and a row for each binary.
  Run {func}`~cogsworth.classify.list_classes` to see the available classes and their criteria.

# Raises

ValueError

: If either `population` is None OR any another parameter is None

functions.name: cogsworth.classify._get_rel_speed
documentation:
Calculate the relative speed of a set of stars at the end of their orbits compared to the circular
velocity at their final positions (given a galactic potential)

# Parameters

orbits

: List of gala Orbits

potential

: The galactic potential used for finding the circular velocity

# Returns

rel_speed

: Relative speed in km / s

functions.name: cogsworth.classify.list_classes
documentation: List out the available classes that are used in other functions

functions.name: cogsworth.classify.get_eddington_rate
documentation:
Get the eddington accretion rate

One of either radiative efficiency or radius must be provided.

# Parameters

mass

: Mass

radiative_efficiency

: Fraction of radiated mass that is accreted, by default None

radius

: Radius, by default None

# Returns

eddington_rate

: Eddington accretion rate

# Raises

ValueError

: If neither radiative efficiency or radius are provided

functions.name: cogsworth.classify.get_eddington_lum
documentation:
Get the eddington luminosity for a given mass

# Parameters

mass

: Mass

# Returns

eddington_lum

: Eddington luminosity

functions.name: cogsworth.classify.get_schwarzchild_radius
documentation:
Get the Schwarzchild radius for a given black hole mass

# Parameters

mass

: Mass

# Returns

r_s

: Schwarzchild radius

functions.name: cogsworth.classify.get_x_ray_lum
documentation:
Estimate the X-ray luminosity for a given binary

We use the prescription from
[Misra+2022](https://ui.adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/2022arXiv220905505M/abstract) to estimate the X-ray
luminosity for both RLOF/wind-fed XRBs and Be-XRBs.

# Parameters

m_acc

: Accretor mass

r_acc

: Accretor radius

m_acc_dot

: Accretion rate

porb

: Orbital period

kstar

: Stellar type

m_don

: Donor mass

RRLO_don

: Donor radius in units of Roche Lobe radius

# Returns

rlof_wind_fed

: Luminosity for a RLOF/wind fed x-ray binary

be_xrb

: Luminosity for Be-XRB

functions.name: cogsworth.observables.get_log_g
documentation:
Computes log of the surface gravity in cgs

# Parameters

mass

: Mass of the star

radius

: Radius of the star

# Returns

log g

: Log of the surface gravity in cgs

functions.name: cogsworth.observables.get_absolute_bol_mag
documentation:
Computes the absolute bolometric magnitude following
[IAU Resolution B2](https://www.iau.org/news/announcements/detail/ann15023/)

# Parameters

lum

: Luminosity of the star

# Returns

M_bol

: Absolute bolometric magnitude

functions.name: cogsworth.observables.get_apparent_mag
documentation:
Convert absolute magnitude to apparent magnitude

# Parameters

M_abs

: Absolute magnitude

distance

: Distance

# Returns

m_app

: Apparent magnitude

functions.name: cogsworth.observables.get_absolute_mag
documentation:
Convert apparent magnitude to absolute magnitude

# Parameters

M_abs

: Apparent magnitude

distance

: Distance

# Returns

m_app

: Absolute magnitude

functions.name: cogsworth.observables.add_mags
documentation:
Add any number of magnitudes

# Parameters

mags

: A series of magnitudes. If arrays are given then all must have the same length. If a mixture of single
  values and arrays are given then the single values will be added to each array element

remove_nans

: Whether to remove NaNs from the total (if not the total will be NaN), by default True

# Returns

total_mag

: Total magnitude

# Raises

ValueError

: If any magnitude is an invalid type

AssertionError

: If any magnitude array has a different length from another

functions.name: cogsworth.observables.get_extinction
documentation:
Calculates the visual extinction values for a set of coordinates

Reddening due to dust is calculated using the Bayestar dustmap. Then the conversion from this to a visual
extension is done assuming a total-to-selective extinction ratio of 3.3, as is used by
[Green+2019](https://iopscience.iop.org/article/10.3847/1538-4357/ab5362#apjab5362s2)

:::{warning}
The dustmap used only covers declinations > -30 degrees, any supplied coordinates below this will be
reflected around the galactic plane (any of [(-l, b), (l, -b), (-l, -b)]) and the dust at these
locations will be used instead
:::

# Parameters

coords

: The coordinates at which you wish to calculate extinction values

# Returns

Av

: Visual extinction values for each set of coordinates

functions.name: cogsworth.observables.get_photometry
documentation:
Computes photometry subject to dust extinction using the MIST boloemtric correction grid

# Parameters

filters

: Which filters to compute photometry for (e.g. ['J', 'H', 'K', 'G', 'BP', 'RP'])

population

: The population for which to compute photometry (either supply this or a final_bpp and final_pos)

final_bpp

: A dataset of COSMIC binaries at present day - must include these columns: ["sep", "metallicity"] and
  for each star it must have the columns ["teff", "lum", "mass", "rad", "kstar"]

final_pos

: Final positions of each system in the galactocentric frame.
  The first `len(self)` entries of each are for bound binaries or primaries, then the final
  `self.disrupted.sum()` entries are for disrupted secondaries. Any missing orbits (where orbit=None
  will be set to `np.inf` for ease of masking.

ignore_extinction

: Whether to ignore extinction

# Returns

photometry

: Photometry and extinction information for supplied COSMIC binaries in desired `filters`

methods.name: cogsworth.tests.test_events.Test.test_various_events
documentation:
Ensure functions work for different kinds of events

I'm going to use fake bpp and kick_info tables for this. There will be four binaries
    - One with no events
    - One with a bound binary and a kick
    - One with a disruption from the first SN
    - One with a disruption from the second SN

Checks for optional dependencies using lazy import from

functions.name: cogsworth.hydro.utils.prepare_snapshot
documentation:
Prepare a snapshot for use in cogsworth

Convert a snapshot to physical units, centre it on the main halo, and orient it face-on and side-on.

# Parameters

path

: Path to the snapshot file or directory (as used in {func}`pynbody.snapshot.load`)

halo_params

: Keyword parameters to be passed to {func}`pynbody.analysis.halo.center`, by default {"mode": "ssc"}

# Returns

snap

: The prepared snapshot

functions.name: cogsworth.hydro.utils.dispersion_from_virial_parameter
documentation:
Calculate the velocity dispersion from the virial parameter

(Using the virial parameter definition from
[Bertoldi & McKee (1992)](https://ui.adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/1992ApJ...395..140B/abstract) Eq. 2.8a)

# Parameters

alpha_vir

: Virial parameter

R

: Cluster radius

M

: Cluster mass

# Returns

v_disp

: Velocity dispersion

functions.name: cogsworth.hydro.potential.get_snapshot_potential
documentation:
Compute the potential of a snapshot of a hydrodynamical zoom-in simulation

# Parameters

snap

: The snapshot

components

: List of components to add to the potential, each component specifies the label to use, attribute in
  the pynbody snap and scale radius to use, by default includes stars, dark matter and gas with scale
  radii of 3, 10 and 3 kpc respectively

out_path

: Path to save the potential (should be a .yml file), by default None

nmax

: Maximum value of \$n\$ for the radial expansion, by default 15

lmax

: Maximum value of \$ell\$ for the spherical harmonics, by default 5

verbose

: Whether to report on progress, by default False

# Returns

pot

: Potential of the snapshot

functions.name: cogsworth.hydro.rewind._tohspans
documentation: tohspans = snapshot backwards in time lol
define a function to integrate the orbit of a particle backwards in time

functions.name: cogsworth.hydro.rewind.rewind_to_formation
documentation:
Rewind a snapshot to the time of formation of each particle

# Parameters

subsnap

: A subset of a snapshot containing only the particles to rewind

pot

: The potential of the snapshot

dt

: Timestep size, by default -1\*u.Myr

processes

: How many processes to use, by default 1 (run single-threaded)

# Returns

init_particles

: A dataframe containing the initial mass, metallicity, formation time, ID,
  position and velocity of each particle

functions.name: cogsworth.kicks.get_kick_differential
documentation:
Calculate the {class}`~astropy.coordinates.CylindricalDifferential` from a combination of the natal
kick, Blauuw kick and orbital motion.

# Parameters

delta_v_sys_xyz

: Change in systemic velocity due to natal and Blauuw kicks in BSE $(v_x, v_y, v_z)$ frame
  (see Fig A1 of [Hurley+02](https://ui.adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/2002MNRAS.329..897H/abstract))

phase

: Orbital phase angle in radians

inclination

: Inclination to the Galactic plane in radians

# Returns

kick_differential

: Kick differential

functions.name: cogsworth.kicks.integrate_orbit_with_events
documentation:
Integrate {class}`~gala.dynamics.PhaseSpacePosition` in a
{class}`Potential <gala.potential.potential.PotentialBase>` with events that occur at certain times

# Parameters

w0

: Initial phase space position

t1

: Integration start time

t2

: Integration end time

dt

: Integration initial timestep size (integrator may adapt timesteps)

potential

: Potential in which you which to integrate the orbits, by default the
  {class}`~gala.potential.potential.MilkyWayPotential`

events

: Events that occur during the orbit evolution (such as supernova resulting in kicks). If no events
  occur then set `events=None` (this will result in a simple call to `potential.integrate_orbit`). If
  this is a disrupted binary then supply a list of 2 lists of events. Each event list should contain
  the following parameters: `time`, `m_1`, `m_2`, `a`, `ecc`, `delta_v_sys_xyz` (and will be passed to
  `get_kick_differential`).

store_all

: Whether to store the entire orbit, by default True. If not then only the final
  PhaseSpacePosition will be stored - this cuts down on memory usage.

quiet

: Whether to silence warning messages about failing orbits

# Returns

full_orbit

: Integrated orbit. If a disrupted binary with two event lists was supplied then two orbit classes will
  be returned. If the orbit integration failed for any reason then None is returned.

methods.name: cogsworth.hydro.pop.HydroPopulation.__init__
documentation:
A population of stars sampled from a hydrodynamical zoom-in snapshot

Each star particle in the snapshot is converted to a (binary) stellar population, sampled with COSMIC,
with initial positions and kinematics determined based on the virial parameter and particle size.

# Parameters

star_particles

: A table of star particles from a snapshot that contains the mass, metallicity, formation time,
  position and velocity of each particle, as returned by
  {func}`~cogsworth.hydro.rewind.rewind_to_formation`

cluster_radius

: Size of gaussian for cluster for each star particle, by default 3 * u.pc

cluster_mass

: Mass of cluster for each star particle for calculating velocity dispersion,
  by default 1e4 * u.Msun

virial_parameter

: Virial parameter for each cluster, for setting velocity dispersions, by default 1.0

subset

: If set, only use a subset of the star particles, by default use all of them. If an integer is
  passed, a random subset of that size is used, if a list of integers is passed, only those IDs are
  used.

sampling_params

: As in {class}`~cogsworth.pop.Population` BUT changing the defaults to
  {"sampling_target": "total_mass", "trim_extra_samples": True} (i.e. to sample to the total mass of
  the star particle)

snapshot_type

: What sort of snapshot this to infer what citations to use, by default None (i.e. don't infer).
  Currently supported: "FIRE"

kwargs

: Parameters to pass to {class}`~cogsworth.pop.Population`

methods.name: cogsworth.hydro.pop.HydroPopulation.sample_initial_binaries
documentation: Sample initial binaries from the star particles in the snapshot

methods.name: cogsworth.hydro.pop.HydroPopulation.perform_stellar_evolution
documentation:
Perform stellar evolution on systems sampled from the star particles
and track their parent particles

modules.name: cogsworth.citations
documentation: storage for citations a dictionary of tuples, first element bibtex id(s), second element bibtex

functions.name: cogsworth.plot.plot_cmd
documentation:
Plot a colour-magnitude diagram for a population.

# Parameters

pop

: Population to plot.

m_filter

: Filter for the magnitude, by default "G"

c_filter_1

: Filter for the first colour, by default "BP"

c_filter_2

: Filter for the second colour, by default "RP"

fig

: Figure on which to plot, by default will create a new one

ax

: Axes on which to plot, by default will create a new one

show

: Whether to immediately show the plot, by default True

# Returns

fig, ax

: Figure and axis that contains the plot

functions.name: cogsworth.plot._use_white_text
documentation:
Determines whether to use white text on a given background color based on the RGBA value.

Parameters
----------
rgba : `tuple`
    RGBA value

Returns
-------
flag : `bool`
    True if white text should be used

functions.name: cogsworth.plot._supernova_marker
documentation:
Add a supernova marker to an ax

# Parameters

ax

: Axis on which to add marker

x

: x coordinate

y

: y coordinate

s

: Size scale

functions.name: cogsworth.plot._rlof_path
documentation:
Draw a path in the shape of RLOF (teardrop)

# Parameters

centre

: Centre of the teardrop (x, y)

width

: How wide to make

height

: How tall to make it

m

: The 'pointy-ness' of the teardrop, by default 1.5

flip

: Whether to flip the direction, by default False

# Returns

x, y

: Path of the teardrop

functions.name: cogsworth.plot.plot_cartoon_evolution
documentation:
Plot COSMIC bpp output as a cartoon evolution

# Parameters

bpp

: COSMIC bpp table

bin_num

: Binary number of the binary to plot

label_type

: What sort of annotated labels to use ["short", "long", "sentence"], by default "long"

plot_title

: Title to use for the plot, use "" for no title, by default "Cartoon Binary Evolution"

y_sep_mult

: Multiplier to use for the y separation (larger=more spread out steps, longer figure)

offset

: Offset from the centre for each of the stars (larger=wider binaries)

s_base

: Base scatter point size for the stars

fig

: Figure on which to plot, by default will create a new one

ax

: Axis on which to plot, by default will create a new one

show

: Whether to immediately show the plot, by default True

# Returns

fig, ax

: Figure and axis of the plot

functions.name: cogsworth.plot.plot_galactic_orbit
documentation:
Plot the galactic orbit of a binary system. This provides a wrapper around the gala
{class}`~gala.dynamics.Orbit` method {meth}`~gala.dynamics.Orbit.plot`.

# Parameters

primary_orbit

: Orbit of the primary star (or bound binary)

secondary_orbit

: Orbit of the secondary star, by default None (no disruption)

t_min

: Minimum time since the start of the orbits to plot, by default 0\*u.Myr

t_max

: Maximum time since the start of the orbits to plot, by default np.inf\*u

show_start

: Whether to plot a marker at the start of the orbits, by default True

primary_kwargs

: Keyword arguments to pass to the primary orbit plot, by default {}

secondary_kwargs

: Keyword arguments to pass to the secondary orbit plot, by default {}

start_kwargs

: Keyword arguments to pass to the start marker plot, by default {}

fig

: Figure on which to plot, by default will create a new one

axes

: Axes on which to plot, by default will create a new one

show

: Whether to immediately show the plot, by default

# Returns

fig, axes

: Figure and axes of the plot

functions.name: cogsworth.sfh.load
documentation:
Load an entire class from storage.

Data should be stored in an hdf5 file using `file_name`.

Parameters
----------
file_name : `str`
    A name of the .h5 file in which samples are stored and .txt file in which parameters are stored
key : `str`, optional
    Key to use for the hdf5 file, by default "sfh"

functions.name: cogsworth.sfh.concat
documentation:
Concatenate multiple StarFormationHistory objects together.

Parameters
----------
*sfhs : `StarFormationHistory`
    Any number of StarFormationHistory objects to concatenate

Returns
-------
`StarFormationHistory`
    A new StarFormationHistory object that is the concatenation of all the input objects

classes.name: cogsworth.sfh.StarFormationHistory

methods.name: cogsworth.sfh.StarFormationHistory.size
documentation: The number of points in the star formation history model

methods.name: cogsworth.sfh.StarFormationHistory.components
documentation:
A list of the components in the star formation history model

# Returns

components

: The list of the components in the star formation history model

methods.name: cogsworth.sfh.StarFormationHistory.component_masses
documentation:
The masses of the components in the star formation history model

# Returns

component_masses

: The masses of the components in the star formation history model

methods.name: cogsworth.sfh.StarFormationHistory.tau
documentation:
The lookback times of the sampled points

# Returns

tau

: The lookback times of the sampled points

methods.name: cogsworth.sfh.StarFormationHistory.Z
documentation:
The metallicities of the sampled points

# Returns

Z

: The metallicities of the sampled points (absolute metallicity **not** solar metallicity)

methods.name: cogsworth.sfh.StarFormationHistory.x
documentation:
The galactocentric x positions of the sampled points

# Returns

x

: The galactocentric x positions of the sampled points

methods.name: cogsworth.sfh.StarFormationHistory.y
documentation:
The galactocentric y positions of the sampled points

# Returns

y

: The galactocentric y positions of the sampled points

methods.name: cogsworth.sfh.StarFormationHistory.z
documentation:
The galactocentric z positions of the sampled points

# Returns

z

: The galactocentric z positions of the sampled points

methods.name: cogsworth.sfh.StarFormationHistory.rho
documentation:
The galactocentric cylindrical radius of the sampled points

A shortcut for the radius in the x-y plane, $\sqrt{x^2 + y^2}$

# Returns

rho

: The galactocentric cylindrical radius of the sampled points

methods.name: cogsworth.sfh.StarFormationHistory.phi
documentation:
The galactocentric azimuthal angle of the sampled points

A shortcut for $\arctan(y / x)$

# Returns

phi

: The galactocentric azimuthal angle of the sampled points

methods.name: cogsworth.sfh.StarFormationHistory.positions
documentation:
The galactocentric positions of the sampled points

# Returns

positions

: The galactocentric positions of the sampled points

methods.name: cogsworth.sfh.StarFormationHistory.which_comp
documentation:
The component each point belongs to

# Returns

which_comp

: The component each point belongs to

methods.name: cogsworth.sfh.StarFormationHistory.get_citations
documentation: Print the citations for the packages/papers used in the star formation history

methods.name: cogsworth.sfh.StarFormationHistory.sample
documentation: Sample from the distributions for each component, combine and save in class attributes

methods.name: cogsworth.sfh.StarFormationHistory.save
documentation:
Save the entire class to storage.

Data will be stored in an hdf5 file using `file_name`.

Parameters
----------
file_name : `str`
    A name to use for the hdf5 file in which samples will be stored. If this doesn't end in ".h5" then
    ".h5" will be appended.
key : `str`, optional
    Key to use for the hdf5 file, by default "sfh"

Class for a generic galactic star formation history model from which to sample

This class sets out an outline for sampling from a star formation history model but a subclass will be
needed for things to function properly.

All attributes listed below are a given value for the sampled points in the galaxy. If one hasn't been
sampled/calculated when accessed then it will be automatically sampled/calculated. If sampling, ALL values
will be sampled.

Parameters
----------
size : `int`
    Number of points to sample from the model
components : `list`, optional
    List of component names, by default None
component_masses : `list`, optional
    List of masses associated with each component (must be the same length as `components`),
    by default None
immediately_sample : `bool`, optional
    Whether to immediately sample the points, by default True

classes.name: cogsworth.sfh.BurstUniformDisc
documentation:
An extremely simple star formation history, with all stars formed at `t_burst` in a uniform disc with
height `z_max` and radius `R_max` disc, all with metallicity `Z`.

# Parameters

size

: Number of points to sample from the model

t_burst

: Lookback time at which all stars are formed

z_max

: Maximum height of the disc

R_max

: Maximum radius of the disc

Z

: Metallicity of the disc, by default 0.02

classes.name: cogsworth.sfh.ConstantUniformDisc
documentation:
A simple star formation history, with all stars formed at a constant rate between `t_burst`
and the present day in a uniform disc with height `z_max` and radius `R_max` disc, all with
metallicity `Z`.

Based on {class}`BurstUniformDisc`.

classes.name: cogsworth.sfh.Wagg2022

methods.name: cogsworth.sfh.Wagg2022.draw_lookback_times
documentation:
Inverse CDF sampling of lookback times. low_alpha and high_alpha discs uses
[Frankel+2018](https://ui.adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/2018ApJ...865...96F/abstract) Eq. 4,
separated and normalised at 8 Gyr. The bulge matches the distribution in Fig. 7 of
[Bovy+19](https://ui.adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/2019MNRAS.490.4740B/abstract) but accounts
for sample's bias.

# Parameters

size

: How many times to draw

component

: Which component of the Milky Way

# Returns

tau

: Random lookback times

methods.name: cogsworth.sfh.Wagg2022.draw_radii
documentation:
Inverse CDF sampling of galactocentric radii using
[Frankel+2018](https://ui.adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/2018ApJ...865...96F/abstract) Eq. 5.
The scale length is calculated using Eq. 6 the low_alpha disc and is fixed at 1/0.43 and 1.5 kpc
respectively for the high_alpha disc and bulge.

# Parameters

size

: How many radii to draw

component

: Which component of the Milky Way

# Returns

rho

: Random Galactocentric radius

methods.name: cogsworth.sfh.Wagg2022.draw_heights
documentation:
Inverse CDF sampling of heights using
[McMillan 2011](https://ui.adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/2011MNRAS.414.2446M/abstract) Eq. 3
and various scale lengths.

# Parameters

size

: How many heights to draw

component

: Which component of the Milky Way

# Returns

z

: Random heights

methods.name: cogsworth.sfh.Wagg2022.draw_phi
documentation:
Draw random azimuthal angles

# Parameters

size

: How many angles to draw, by default self.\_size

# Returns

phi

: Azimuthal angles

methods.name: cogsworth.sfh.Wagg2022.get_metallicity
documentation:
Convert radius and time to metallicity using
[Frankel+2018](https://ui.adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/2018ApJ...865...96F/abstract) Eq. 7 and
[Bertelli+1994](https://ui.adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/1994A%26AS..106..275B/abstract) Eq. 9 but
assuming all stars have the solar abundance pattern (so no factor of 0.977)

# Returns

Z

: Metallicities corresponding to radii and times

A semi-empirical model defined in
[Wagg+2022](https://ui.adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/2021arXiv211113704W/abstract)
(see Figure 1 and Section 2.2.1 for a detailed explanation.), heavily based on
[Frankel+2018](https://ui.adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/2018ApJ...865...96F/abstract).

Parameters are the same as {class}`StarFormationHistory` but additionally with the following:

# Parameters

galaxy_age

: Maximum lookback time, by default 12\*u.Gyr

tsfr

: Star formation timescale, by default 6.8\*u.Gyr

alpha

: Disc inside-out growth parameter, by default 0.3

Fm

: Metallicity at centre of disc at tm, by default -1

gradient

: Metallicity gradient, by default -0.075/u.kpc

Rnow

: Radius at which present day metallicity is solar, by default 8.7\*u.kpc

gamma

: Time dependence of chemical enrichment, by default 0.3

zsun

: Solar metallicity, by default 0.0142

classes.name: cogsworth.sfh.QuasiIsothermalDisk

methods.name: cogsworth.sfh.QuasiIsothermalDisk.draw_lookback_times
documentation:
Inverse CDF sampling of lookback times. low_alpha and high_alpha discs uses
[Frankel+2018](https://ui.adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/2018ApJ...865...96F/abstract) Eq. 4,
separated and normalised at 8 Gyr. The bulge matches the distribution in Fig. 7 of
[Bovy+19](https://ui.adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/2019MNRAS.490.4740B/abstract) but accounts
for sample's bias.

# Parameters

size

: How many times to draw

component

: Which component of the Milky Way

# Returns

tau

: Random lookback times

methods.name: cogsworth.sfh.QuasiIsothermalDisk.get_metallicity
documentation:
Convert radius and time to metallicity using
[Frankel+2018](https://ui.adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/2018ApJ...865...96F/abstract) Eq. 7 and
[Bertelli+1994](https://ui.adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/1994A%26AS..106..275B/abstract) Eq. 9 but
assuming all stars have the solar abundance pattern (so no factor of 0.977)

# Returns

Z

: Metallicities corresponding to radii and times

methods.name: cogsworth.sfh.QuasiIsothermalDisk.get_DF
documentation: Get the distribution function for a quasi-isothermal disk based on the Gala MW potential

methods.name: cogsworth.sfh.QuasiIsothermalDisk.sample
documentation: Sample from the Galaxy distribution and save in class attributes

A quasi-isothermal distribution function with parameters from
[Sanders & Binney 2015](https://ui.adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/2015MNRAS.449.3479S/abstract).

Parameters are the same as {class}`StarFormationHistory` but additionally with the following:

# Parameters

galaxy_age

: Maximum lookback time, by default 12\*u.Gyr

tsfr

: Star formation timescale, by default 6.8\*u.Gyr

Fm

: Metallicity at centre of disc at tm, by default -1

gradient

: Metallicity gradient, by default -0.075/u.kpc

Rnow

: Radius at which present day metallicity is solar, by default 8.7\*u.kpc

gamma

: Time dependence of chemical enrichment, by default 0.3

zsun

: Solar metallicity, by default 0.0142

classes.name: cogsworth.sfh.SpheroidalDwarf

methods.name: cogsworth.sfh.SpheroidalDwarf.draw_lookback_times
documentation:
Inverse CDF sampling of lookback times. low_alpha and high_alpha discs uses
[Frankel+2018](https://ui.adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/2018ApJ...865...96F/abstract) Eq. 4,
separated and normalised at 8 Gyr. The bulge matches the distribution in Fig. 7 of
[Bovy+19](https://ui.adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/2019MNRAS.490.4740B/abstract) but accounts
for sample's bias.

# Parameters

size

: How many times to draw

component

: Which component of the Milky Way

# Returns

tau

: Random lookback times

methods.name: cogsworth.sfh.SpheroidalDwarf.get_metallicity
documentation:
Convert radius and time to metallicity using
[Frankel+2018](https://ui.adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/2018ApJ...865...96F/abstract) Eq. 7 and
[Bertelli+1994](https://ui.adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/1994A%26AS..106..275B/abstract) Eq. 9 but
assuming all stars have the solar abundance pattern (so no factor of 0.977)

# Returns

Z

: Metallicities corresponding to radii and times

methods.name: cogsworth.sfh.SpheroidalDwarf.get_DF
documentation: Get the distribution function for a dwarf galaxy disk based on an NFW profile

methods.name: cogsworth.sfh.SpheroidalDwarf.sample
documentation: Sample from the Galaxy distribution and save in class attributes

An action-based model for dwarf spheroidal galaxies and globular clusters
[Pascale+2019](https://ui.adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/2019MNRAS.488.2423P/abstract).

Parameters are the same as {class}`StarFormationHistory` but additionally with the following:

# Parameters

mass

: Total mass of the galactic potential

J_0_star

: The action scale that naturally defines the length- and velocity-scale

alpha

: A non-negative, dimensionless parameter that mainly regulates the models density profile

eta

: A non-negative, dimensionless parameter that mainly controls the radial or tangential bias of the
  model velocity distribution; models sharing the parameters \$(alpha, eta)\$ are homologous.

galaxy_age

: Maximum lookback time, by default 12\*u.Gyr

tsfr

: Star formation timescale, by default 6.8\*u.Gyr

Fm

: Metallicity at centre of disc at tm, by default -1

gradient

: Metallicity gradient, by default -0.075/u.kpc

Rnow

: Radius at which present day metallicity is solar, by default 8.7\*u.kpc

gamma

: Time dependence of chemical enrichment, by default 0.3

zsun

: Solar metallicity, by default 0.0142

functions.name: cogsworth.pop.load
documentation:
Load a Population from a series of files

Parameters
----------
file_name : `str`
    Base name of the files to use. Should either have no file extension or ".h5"
parts : `list`, optional
    Which parts of the Population to load immediately, the rest are loaded as necessary. Any of
    ["initial_binaries", "initial_galaxy", "stellar_evolution", "galactic_orbits"], by default
    ["initial_binaries", "initial_galaxy", "stellar_evolution"]

Returns
-------
pop : `Population`
    The loaded Population

functions.name: cogsworth.pop.concat
documentation:
Concatenate multiple populations into a single population

NOTE: The final population will have the same settings as the first population in the list (but data
from all populations)

# Parameters

pops

: List of populations to concatenate

# Returns

total_pop

: The concatenated population

classes.name: cogsworth.pop.Population

methods.name: cogsworth.pop.Population.copy
documentation: Create a copy of the population

methods.name: cogsworth.pop.Population.get_citations
documentation: Print the citations for the packages/papers used in the population

methods.name: cogsworth.pop.Population.bin_nums
documentation:
An array of the unique identifiers for each binary in a population.

This is a helper function which pulls from the bin_nums available in {attr}`final_bpp`,
or {attr}`initC`, or {attr}`initial_binaries` if available. If none of these are available, an
error is raised.

# Returns

bin_nums

: An array of the unique identifiers for each binary in the population.

# Raises

ValueError

: If no binaries have been sampled or evolved yet.

methods.name: cogsworth.pop.Population.initial_galaxy
documentation:
The initial sampled galactic star formation history.

This contains the initial conditions for the galaxy that was sampled to generate the population. This
includes the birth times, positions, velocities, and metallicities of the stars in the galaxy.

# Returns

initial_galaxy

: The initial galaxy that was sampled to generate the population.

# Raises

ValueError

: If no galaxy has been sampled yet.

methods.name: cogsworth.pop.Population.mass_singles
documentation:
The total mass in single stars needed to generate the population.

Returns
-------
mass_singles : `float`
    The total mass in single stars needed to generate the population (in solar masses).

Raises
------
ValueError
    If no population sampled yet.

methods.name: cogsworth.pop.Population.mass_binaries
documentation:
The total mass in binaries needed to generate the population.

Returns
-------
mass_binaries : `float`
    The total mass in binaries

Raises
------
ValueError
    If no population sampled yet.

methods.name: cogsworth.pop.Population.n_singles_req
documentation:
The number of single stars needed to generate the population.

Returns
-------
n_singles_req : `int`
    The number of single stars needed to generate the population.

Raises
------
ValueError
    If no population sampled yet.

methods.name: cogsworth.pop.Population.n_bin_req
documentation:
The number of binary stars needed to generate the population.

Returns
-------
n_bin_req : `int`
    The number of binary stars needed to generate the population.

Raises
------
ValueError
    If no population sampled yet.

methods.name: cogsworth.pop.Population.initial_binaries
documentation:
The initial binaries that were sampled to generate the population.

This is only used when evolution has not been performed yet. If evolution has been performed, the
initial binaries are stored in the {attr}`initC` table instead (since this also includes information
on the assumed binary physics).

# Returns

initial_binaries

: The initial binaries that were sampled to generate the population.

# Raises

ValueError

: If no binaries have been sampled yet.

methods.name: cogsworth.pop.Population.bpp
documentation:
A table of the evolutionary history of each binary.

Each row of this table corresponds to an important timestep in the evolution of a binary. Events that
log a timestep include: stellar type of a star changing, mass transfer, a supernova, a common envelope
event, a binary merger, or a disruption. The columns of the table are described in detail [in the
COSMIC documentation](https://cosmic-popsynth.github.io/docs/stable/output_info/index.html#bpp).

# Returns

bpp

: The evolutionary history of each binary.

# Raises

ValueError

: If no stellar evolution has been performed yet.

methods.name: cogsworth.pop.Population.bcm
documentation:
A table of the evolutionary history of each binary at dynamically chosen timesteps.

Each row of this table corresponds to a timestep in the evolution of a binary. Timesteps are set
based on user-defined `bcm_timestep_conditions`. The columns of the table are described in
detail
[in the COSMIC documentation](https://cosmic-popsynth.github.io/docs/stable/output_info/index.html#bcm).

# Returns

bcm

: The evolutionary history of each binary at dynamically chosen timesteps. Note this will be
  `None` if no timestep conditions have been set (in `bcm_timestep_conditions`).

# Raises

Warning

: If no timestep conditions (`bcm_timestep_condition`) have been set for the BCM table.

ValueError

: If no stellar evolution has been performed yet.

methods.name: cogsworth.pop.Population.initC
documentation:
A table of initial conditions for each binary.

This table contains the initial conditions for each binary that was sampled. Each row corresponds to
a binary and the columns are described in detail in the COSMIC documentation.

# Returns

initC

: The initial conditions for each binary.

# Raises

ValueError

: If no stellar evolution has been performed yet.

methods.name: cogsworth.pop.Population.kick_info
documentation:
A table of the kicks that occur for each binary.

Each row of this table corresponds to a potential supernova kick, such that there are two rows for
each binary in the population. The columns of the table are described in detail
[in the COSMIC documentation](https://cosmic-popsynth.github.io/docs/stable/output_info/index.html#kick-info).

# Returns

kick_info

: Information about the kicks that occur for each binary.

# Raises

ValueError

: If no stellar evolution has been performed yet.

methods.name: cogsworth.pop.Population.orbits
documentation:
The orbits of each system within the galaxy from its birth until {attr}`max_ev_time`.

This list will have length = `len(self) + self.disrupted.sum()`, where the first section are for
bound binaries and disrupted primaries and the last section are for disrupted secondaries.

# Returns

orbits

: The orbits of each system within the galaxy from its birth until {attr}`max_ev_time`.

# Raises

ValueError

: If no orbits have been calculated yet.

methods.name: cogsworth.pop.Population.primary_orbits
documentation:
The orbits of primary stars

# Returns

primary_orbits

: The orbits of the primary stars in the population

methods.name: cogsworth.pop.Population.secondary_orbits
documentation:
The orbits of secondary stars

# Returns

secondary_orbits

: The orbits of the secondary stars in the population

methods.name: cogsworth.pop.Population.classes
documentation:
A table of classes that apply to each binary.

Each row corresponds to a binary and the columns are a boolean of whether they meet the criteria for
a class. For a full description of classes and their criteria,
run {func}`~cogsworth.classify.list_classes`.

# Returns

classes

: The classes that apply to each binary.

methods.name: cogsworth.pop.Population.final_pos
documentation:
The final position of each binary (or star from a disrupted binary) in the galaxy.

# Returns

final_pos

: The final position of each binary (or star from a disrupted binary) in the galaxy.

methods.name: cogsworth.pop.Population.final_vel
documentation:
The final velocity of each binary (or star from a disrupted binary) in the galaxy.

# Returns

final_vel

: The final velocity of each binary (or star from a disrupted binary) in the galaxy.

methods.name: cogsworth.pop.Population.final_bpp
documentation:
The final state of each binary in the population.

This is simply the final row from the {attr}`bpp` table for each binary.

# Returns

final_bpp

: The final state of each binary in the population.

methods.name: cogsworth.pop.Population.disrupted
documentation:
A mask of whether a binary was disrupted during its evolution.

# Returns

disrupted

: A mask of whether a binary was disrupted during its evolution.

methods.name: cogsworth.pop.Population.escaped
documentation:
A mask of whether a binary escaped the galaxy during its evolution.

This is calculated by comparing the final velocity of the binary to the escape velocity at its
final position.

# Returns

escaped

: A mask of whether a binary escaped the galaxy during its evolution.

methods.name: cogsworth.pop.Population.observables
documentation:
A table of the observable properties of each binary.

# Returns

observables

: The observable properties of each binary. Columns are defined in
  {func}`~cogsworth.observables.get_observables`.

# Raises

ValueError

: If no observables have been calculated yet.

methods.name: cogsworth.pop.Population.create_population
documentation:
Create an entirely evolved population of binaries.

This will sample the initial binaries and initial galaxy and then perform both the {py:mod}`cosmic`
and {py:mod}`gala` evolution.

# Parameters

with_timing

: Whether to print messages about the timing, by default True

methods.name: cogsworth.pop.Population.sample_initial_galaxy
documentation: Sample the initial galactic times, positions and velocities

methods.name: cogsworth.pop.Population.sample_initial_binaries
documentation:
Sample the initial binary parameters for the population.

Alternatively, copy initial conditions from another population

# Parameters

initC

: Initial conditions from a different Population, by default None (new sampling performed)

overwrite_initC_settings

: Whether to overwrite initC settings in the case where the new population has a different set of
  `BSE_settings`, by default True

reset_sampled_kicks

: Whether to reset any sampled kicks in the population to ensure new ones are drawn, by default True

methods.name: cogsworth.pop.Population.perform_stellar_evolution
documentation: Perform the (binary) stellar evolution of the sampled binaries

methods.name: cogsworth.pop.Population.perform_galactic_evolution
documentation:
Use {py:mod}`gala` to perform the orbital integration for each evolved binary

# Parameters

quiet

: Whether to silence any warnings about failing orbits, by default False

methods.name: cogsworth.pop.Population._get_final_coords
documentation:
Get the final coordinates of each binary (or each component in disrupted binaries)

# Returns

final_pos, final_vel

: Final positions and velocities of each system in the galactocentric frame.
  The first `len(self)` entries of each are for bound binaries or primaries, then the final
  `self.disrupted.sum()` entries are for disrupted secondaries. Any missing orbits (where orbit=None
  will be set to `np.inf` for ease of masking.

methods.name: cogsworth.pop.Population.get_observables
documentation:
Get observables associated with the binaries at present day.

These include: extinction due to dust, absolute and apparent bolometric magnitudes for each star,
apparent magnitudes in each filter and observed temperature and surface gravity for each binary.

For bound binaries and stellar mergers, only the column `{filter}_app_1` is relevant. For
disrupted binaries, `{filter}_app_1` is for the primary star and `{filter}_app_2` is for
the secondary star.

# Parameters

kwargs to pass to {func}`~cogsworth.observables.get_photometry`

methods.name: cogsworth.pop.Population.get_gaia_observed_bin_nums
documentation:
Get a list of `bin_nums` of systems that are bright enough to be observed by Gaia.

This is calculated based on the Gaia selection function provided by {mod}`gaiaunlimited`. This
function returns a **random sample** of systems where systems are included in the observed subset
with a probability given by Gaia's completeness at their location.

E.g. if Gaia's completeness is 0 for a source of a given magnitude and location then it will never be
included. Similarly, if the completeness is 1 then it will always be included. However, if the
completeness is 0.5 then it will only be included in the list of `bin_nums` half of the time.

# Returns

primary_observed

: A list of binary numbers (that can be used in tables like {attr}`final_bpp`) for which the
  bound binary/disrupted primary would be observed

secondary_observed

: A list of binary numbers (that can be used in tables like {attr}`final_bpp`) for which the
  disrupted secondary would be observed

methods.name: cogsworth.pop.Population.get_final_mw_skycoord
documentation:
Get the final positions and velocities as an astropy SkyCoord object

..warning:

```
This function assumes the final positions and velocities are in the MW galactocentric frame
```

methods.name: cogsworth.pop.Population.plot_sky_locations
documentation: Plot the final positions of the binaries in the Milky Way galactocentric frame

methods.name: cogsworth.pop.Population.get_healpix_inds
documentation:
Get the indices of the healpix pixels that each binary is in

# Parameters

ra

: Either the right ascension of the system in radians or "auto" to automatically calculate assuming
  Milky Way galactocentric coordinates

dec

: Either the declination of the system in radians or "auto" to automatically calculate assuming
  Milky Way galactocentric coordinates

nside

: Healpix nside parameter, by default 128

# Returns

pix

: The indices for each system

methods.name: cogsworth.pop.Population.plot_map
documentation:
Plot a healpix map of the final positions of all binaries in population

# Parameters

nside

: Healpix nside parameter, by default 128

coord

: Which coordinates to plot. One of \["C[elestial]", "G\[alactic", "E[quatorial]"\], by default "C"

cmap

: A [matplotlib colormap](https://matplotlib.org/stable/gallery/color/colormap_reference.html)
  to use for the plot, by default "magma"

norm

: How to normalise the total number of binaries (anything linear or log), by default "log"

unit

: A label to use for the unit of the colour bar, by default "$\log_{10}(N_{\rm binaries})$"
  if `norm==log` and "$N_{\rm binaries}$" if `norm==linear`

show

: Whether to immediately show the plot, by default True

mollview_kwargs

: Any additional arguments that you want to pass to healpy's
  [mollview](https://healpy.readthedocs.io/en/latest/generated/healpy.visufunc.mollview.html)

methods.name: cogsworth.pop.Population.translate_tables
documentation:
Translate the COSMIC BSE tables to human readable format

# Parameters

kwargs

: Any arguments to pass to {func}`~cogsworth.utils.translate_COSMIC_tables`

methods.name: cogsworth.pop.Population.plot_cartoon_binary
documentation:
Plot a cartoon of the evolution of a single binary

# Parameters

bin_num

: Which binary to plot

kwargs

: Keyword arguments to pass, see {func}`~cogsworth.plot.plot_cartoon_evolution` for options

# Returns

fig, ax

: Figure and axis of the plot

methods.name: cogsworth.pop.Population.plot_orbit
documentation:
Plot the Galactic orbit of a binary system

# Parameters

bin_num

: Which binary to plot

show_sn

: Whether to show the position of the SN, by default True

sn_kwargs

: Any additional arguments to pass to the SN scatter plot call, by default {}

show

: Whether to immediately show the plot

show_legend

: Whether to show the legend

kwargs

: Any additional arguments to pass to {func}`cogsworth.plot.plot_galactic_orbit`

# Returns

fig, axes

: Figure and axes of the plot

# Raises

ValueError

: If the `bin_num` isn't in the population

methods.name: cogsworth.pop.Population.to_legwork_sources
documentation:
Convert the final state of the population to a LEGWORK Source class (only including bound binaries)

# Parameters

distances

: Custom distance to each source, by default None

assume_mw_galactocentric

: Assume population is in Milky Way and galactocentric coordinate system - such that distances can
  be calculated assuming the present location of the Earth, by default False

# Returns

sources

: LEGWORK sources

# Raises

ValueError

: When distances aren't provided and assume_mw_galactocentric=False

methods.name: cogsworth.pop.Population.save
documentation:
Save a Population to disk as an HDF5 file.

Parameters
----------
file_name : `str`
    A file name to use. Either no file extension or ".h5".
overwrite : `bool`, optional
    Whether to overwrite any existing files, by default False

Raises
------
FileExistsError
    If `overwrite=False` and files already exist

Class for creating and evolving populations of binaries throughout the Milky Way

# Parameters

n_binaries

: How many binaries to sample for the population

processes

: How many processes to run if you want multithreading, by default 8

m1_cutoff

: The minimum allowed primary mass, by default 0

final_kstar1

: Desired final types for primary star, by default list(range(16))

final_kstar2

: Desired final types for secondary star, by default list(range(16))

sfh_model

: A StarFormationHistory class to use for sampling the initial galaxy parameters, by default
  {class}`~cogsworth.sfh.Wagg2022`

sfh_params

: Any additional parameters to pass to your chosen `SFH model` when it is initialised

galactic_potential

: Galactic potential to use for evolving the orbits of binaries, by default
  {class}`~gala.potential.potential.MilkyWayPotential`

v_dispersion

: Velocity dispersion to apply relative to the local circular velocity, by default 5\*u.km/u.s

max_ev_time

: Maximum evolution time for both COSMIC and Gala, by default 12.0\*u.Gyr

timestep_size

: Size of timesteps to use in galactic evolution, by default 1\*u.Myr

BSE_settings

: Any BSE settings to pass to COSMIC, superseded by `ini_file` if provided

ini_file

: Path to an ini file to use for the COSMIC stellar evolution, supersedes `BSE_settings` by default None

bcm_timestep_conditions

: Any timestep conditions to pass to COSMIC evolution. This will affect the rows that are output in the
  the BCM table, by default only the first and last timesteps are output. For more details check out the
  [relevant COSMIC docs](https://cosmic-popsynth.github.io/COSMIC/examples/index.html#dynamically-set-time-resolution-for-bcm-array)

sampling_params

: Any additional parameters to pass to the COSMIC sampling (see
  {meth}`~cosmic.sample.sampler.independent.get_independent_sampler`)

store_entire_orbits

: Whether to store the entire orbit for each binary, by default True. If not then only the final
  PhaseSpacePosition will be stored. This cuts down on both memory usage and disk space used if you
  save the Population (as well as how long it takes to reload the data).

methods.name: cogsworth.pop.EvolvedPopulation.create_population
documentation:
Create an entirely evolved population of binaries with sampling or stellar evolution

This will sample the initial galaxy and then perform the {py:mod}`gala` evolution.

# Parameters

with_timing

: Whether to print messages about the timing, by default True
